skill.name: next-js-16-launchpad
skill.description: Guides Next.js 16 projects through setup, migration, App Router patterns, Turbopack defaults, Cache Components, and proxy.ts usage.
skill.summary: Build, migrate, and validate Next.js 16 applications with App Router, Turbopack, Cache Components, and proxy.ts.
skill.author: Calel33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and sensitive-data patterns, but most are Markdown examples or public starter-code samples. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill should publish with a medium warning because the included PowerShell bootstrap script and documented commands execute package-manager operations when users run them.

medium_findings.title: Bootstrap Script Executes Package Manager Commands
medium_findings.description: The PowerShell helper runs npx and npm commands, including a codemod, package installation, and create-next-app. This is visible setup behavior, not hidden malware, but it fetches and runs external packages and writes project files when executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes npx and npm, so execution risk is real. Confidence is below very high because the behavior is documented bootstrap functionality rather than concealed or malicious activity.

medium_findings.title: Command-Heavy Documentation Requires User Review
medium_findings.description: The static command findings mostly come from Markdown examples for npx, npm, node, next, and mv. These are false positives for hidden code execution, but users may copy and run them, so publication should include a warning about reviewing commands first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is Markdown code blocks and checklist text, not hidden runtime execution. Risk remains moderate because the commands install or execute external packages if a user follows them.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are Public Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch and hardcoded URL findings point to example endpoints such as jsonplaceholder and api.example.com. I did not find evidence that credentials or local data are sent to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited calls fetch public placeholder content and documentation examples. No secret, environment value, cookie, or file content is included in those requests.

low_findings.title: Environment Secret Access Appears Only in Auth Examples
low_findings.description: The process.env.JWT_SECRET hits are documentation examples for JWT verification. They do not show exfiltration, logging, or hidden collection of environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is used as a JWT verification secret in illustrative code. I found no network send, file write, or secret disclosure path attached to it.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage and Credential Findings Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The browser storage and credential-related hits are explanatory text about when Client Components need browser APIs and why cached scopes cannot access cookies. No browser credential file access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose and table text, not code that opens browser credential stores. This is a strong false positive from keyword matching.

low_findings.title: Starter Error Boundary Displays Error Messages
low_findings.description: The starter dashboard error boundary renders error.message. This is a minor information-disclosure concern if copied into production without sanitization, but it is normal sample UI and not a malicious pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly displays the error message, which can expose implementation details. Severity and confidence are limited because production Next.js behavior and project error contents determine real impact.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections align with framework documentation phrases such as Cache Components, layout metadata, or ordering examples. I did not find MD5, SHA1, DES, or custom cryptographic operations in the inspected locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines do not contain cryptographic algorithms. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated words in metadata or code examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bootstrap Script Executes Package Manager Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The PowerShell helper runs npx and npm commands, including a codemod, package installation, and create-next-app. This is visible setup behavior, not hidden malware, but it fetches and runs external packages and writes project files when executed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes npx and npm, so execution risk is real. Confidence is below very high because the behavior is documented bootstrap functionality rather than concealed or malicious activity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Calls Are Public Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The fetch and hardcoded URL findings point to example endpoints such as jsonplaceholder and api.example.com. I did not find evidence that credentials or local data are sent to an unauthorized endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited calls fetch public placeholder content and documentation examples. No secret, environment value, cookie, or file content is included in those requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Secret Access Appears Only in Auth Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The process.env.JWT_SECRET hits are documentation examples for JWT verification. They do not show exfiltration, logging, or hidden collection of environment variables.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is used as a JWT verification secret in illustrative code. I found no network send, file write, or secret disclosure path attached to it.

content.value_statement: Next.js 16 migrations include breaking routing, caching, and tooling changes. This skill gives focused workflows, starter assets, and review checklists for modern App Router projects.
actual_capabilities: Explains Next.js 16 setup requirements for Node.js, TypeScript, React, and App Router projects.
Provides migration guidance for async request APIs, middleware to proxy.ts, and removed config flags.
Documents Cache Components patterns with use cache, cacheLife, cacheTag, Suspense, and revalidation guidance.
Includes a starter App Router reference app with pages, dashboard routing, loading, error, and proxy examples.
Supplies a PowerShell bootstrap script for installing packages and creating a recommended Next.js project.
Highlights common pitfalls for Turbopack, images, runtime request data, and deployment settings.
limitations: The guide targets Next.js 16 and may not fit Pages Router or older Next.js projects.
The bootstrap script runs package-manager commands and should be reviewed before execution.
Example API URLs and auth snippets are illustrative and must be replaced for production use.
The skill does not validate third-party package security or project-specific dependency risk.

use_cases.title: Start a new App Router project
use_cases.description: Create a Next.js 16 baseline with Turbopack, TypeScript, Tailwind, and App Router defaults.

use_cases.title: Plan a version upgrade
use_cases.description: Map required changes from Next.js 15 to Next.js 16 before assigning migration work.

use_cases.title: Review caching and routing patterns
use_cases.description: Check Cache Components, Suspense boundaries, proxy.ts auth, and data fetching patterns for correctness.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Next.js 16 starter plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use next-js-16-launchpad to outline a new Next.js 16 project setup with TypeScript, App Router, Turbopack, Tailwind, and basic folder structure.

prompt_templates.title: Upgrade my project checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use next-js-16-launchpad to create a migration checklist for upgrading my Next.js 15 App Router project to Next.js 16.

prompt_templates.title: Review caching architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use next-js-16-launchpad to review my Cache Components design, Suspense boundaries, revalidation strategy, and runtime data handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design a production route pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use next-js-16-launchpad to design a production dashboard pattern with proxy.ts auth, parallel data fetching, Cache Components, and deployment checks.

output_examples.input: I need to migrate from Next.js 15 to Next.js 16.
output: A prioritized migration checklist covering codemods, async request APIs, proxy.ts changes, and removed config options.
A testing plan for Turbopack builds, route behavior, image configuration, and caching boundaries.

output_examples.input: I am building a product page with cached reviews.
output: A route design that separates static shell content from dynamic sections.
Guidance for using Cache Components, cache tags, Suspense, and revalidation without mixing runtime request data into cached scopes.

output_examples.input: I want a secure dashboard route in Next.js 16.
output: A proxy.ts-based access-control plan with server-side session checks.
Notes about replacing sample token handling with production authentication and secret management.

best_practices: Review the bootstrap script before running it because it installs packages and creates project files.
Replace sample API endpoints, cookie names, and JWT examples with production-approved services and secrets handling.
Use the migration checklist with tests for routing, caching, image handling, and Turbopack build behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not run the PowerShell bootstrap script in a sensitive repository without reviewing the package commands first.
Do not copy sample auth snippets directly into production without validation, rotation, and secure cookie settings.
Do not enable Cache Components around cookies, headers, or other runtime request data.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps create or migrate Next.js 16 applications using App Router, Turbopack, Cache Components, and proxy.ts.

faq.question: Can it upgrade an existing project automatically?
faq.answer: It includes guidance and a bootstrap script, but users should review commands before running them.

faq.question: Does it support Pages Router projects?
faq.answer: It focuses on App Router and Next.js 16 workflows. Pages Router coverage is limited to migration context.

faq.question: Are the network URLs production-ready?
faq.answer: No. The URLs are examples for documentation and starter code. Replace them with approved project endpoints.

faq.question: Does the skill include security guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers server-only data, proxy-based auth, input validation, and environment variable boundaries.

faq.question: What should I verify after using it?
faq.answer: Verify package changes, routing behavior, cache boundaries, Turbopack compatibility, and deployment settings.
